chunks.text: The infrastructure funding and financing Amendment Bill. As I was just saying, We've had 62 written submissions and possibly about 20 oral submissions, starting today and possibly on to next Wednesday as well. We're starting via zoom with the Hamilton City Council. All organisations will be given 10 minutes. We've got Greg castings from HCC, good morning, good afternoon.

Can we see you? Good afternoon. I can hear you. I can see you. You can't see me. No, okay, maybe I'll just continue. Yep. Maybe just Yep, proceed. Thank you for your patience. We're running a little bit late, but Greg, if you we've got your submission, so if you can give us some introductory remarks, and we'll open it up to the floor if there's members who want to ask questions or see clarity. So welcome.

Thank you, Kiri and members of the committee. Thanks for the opportunity to speak. Today. I'm appearing on behalf of the Hamilton City Council. I am Greg Karstens, the director of commercial and analytics. So I lead infrastructure funding and financing for Hamilton Council, including private developer agreements, development contributions, alternative funding mechanisms and growth analytics. Hamilton City Council supports the infrastructure funding and financing amendment bill because it strengthens and simplifies the IFF regime and is expected to enable faster delivery of growth infrastructure. We also support the broader scope to fund transport water and community infrastructure and the waters entities and the building systems improvements, I think those are important amendments. Hamilton is New Zealand's fastest growing city, and projected to continue to pay to grow at pace. Further, Hamilton has significant development planned along its boundary outside the city. These planned developments will be functionally reliant on Hamilton's infrastructure networks. In fact, there are eight fast pack track proposals along the boundary and three further seeking referral that collectively represents 10,000 new homes, approximately projected and more than 500 hectares of industrial land, and those several of those major applications are expected to be lodged in the next few months With the Ministry for the Environment. For these reasons, Hamilton Council is seeking clear cost cross boundary IFF mechanisms and aligned with the development Levy and targeted rate reforms. And I just note that Hamilton has made a related submission on the development Levy's exposure draft relating to cross boundary development zooming out a bit as growth continues at pace. Growth infrastructure, as we all know, is becoming increasingly unaffordable. Hamilton has a $2 billion growth related programme over the next 10 years, and existing tools structurally under recover costs from growth for development occurring outside our boundary, but inside our inside, sorry, alongside our boundary, outside Our jurisdiction, we must rely on bespoke commercial agreements, which exacerbates risk and under recovery, the IFF regime, including development levies and targeted rates reform is therefore particularly important to Hamilton City, our ratepayers and business and investment and we urge that the IFF be viewed as a set, as I think it is. But just to reiterate that point, and that its effectiveness depends on how well it integrates with those other tools, we appreciate the committee's work to improve the IFF and it's other work in the infrastructure funding space, and we welcome the opportunity to provide further feedback on these matters in due course. So thank you, and happy to take questions if there are some.

Okay. Thank you. Mr. Carstens, Have we got any questions for Hamilton City Council? Dr Wilson, yeah, thank you, Blair. Just a quick question. Can you provide us with an example of how you've used the IFF in the past to broaden your financing base? Sure, there's a, there's a an IFF being put in place, and in fact, closing in on the finish line currently, which is in the TIA wa lakes area, north of the city, so that will fund significant internal infrastructure within the TIA wa lakes development, which has circa 1500 houses proposed there. We've had, we've had a number of engagements with what was CIP now, nifco on previous developments. So we are quite familiar with the how that tool works. And also we, we wish to use it, and there are use cases for it, and I, and I'm in content, I'm in contact with nifco, so I hope that answers your question.

Okay, very good. Any, any other questions for Mr. Carson? Okay, no, Greg, we'll leave it there. Thank you. Appearing before us. Well, it vocally appearing before us, and hopefully next time I get to see you, thank you. Okay. Thanks for that. Okay. Hora, whenua District Council have withdrawn? Are we? Have we got my Casey? There we go TOA. Oh, thank you, Mike for being ready to go even though you're not the 20 minutes. So appreciate that you've also got kau here. We've got excited from rewiring Aotearoa, and we look forward

to it, great. So I'll take the submission as read. This is about hopefully adopting the ratepayers assistance scheme into this bill. I'm very, very excited about the opportunity for this to happen, mainly because I think this could be a massive economic for the whole of New Zealand across the board. And essentially, the big win for me today is convincing the select committee that small solar and battery installations are indeed part of infrastructure, and the ability for that to bring down future infrastructure costs is super important. Along with that, enabling the ratepayers assistance scheme will generate huge amounts of private investment in the infrastructure that we're going to need, and ultimately lower energy costs for New Zealanders, which I'm sort of something that we are all very aware of, is a massive issue at the moment. So this is what we're essentially our submission is about, is asking for the ratepayers assistance scheme to be included in this bill. By doing so, we'll save the typical household in New Zealand that takes this offer up about $2,000 a year in household bills. And the bigger thing around why this is such a good argument for infrastructure is that, essentially, if 80% of the homes in New Zealand were able to adopt rooftop solar, the biggest barrier to entry being the upfront capital cost, the rate paid. Assistance scheme alleviates that through easily accessible and universal finance, which is super exciting, but 80% of the homes having rooftop solar result in another 40% generation for New Zealand's electricity system. Ika has talked about saving up to $3 billion in infrastructure through flexibility, which also includes the adoption of batteries and homes, which is also massively important for climate adaptation, because the power lines keep falling down in storms. So I wanted to speak for a couple of minutes just to introduce that, but really, I thought the best value of time would be any questions that you guys might have about that. And David kau who's my head of policy at rewiring, is here. He's got a really long adjusting stick when it comes to the technicalities of the ratepayers assistance scheme, so if there's any questions around the mechanics of it. I will be reverting to him. That's it for me.

Okay, thanks, Mike. We're going to go to kau. Did you Dave? Sorry, did you have anything to add at this point? Nothing. That's good. No. I just thought you were about to burst into print. We're going to go to CD. Wade, brown, Kia, ora. Nice to see you, Mike and Dave. Understand the overall benefits, both to the individual and to the country. so I might jump in there. Celia, thank you for your question. A very high level what the four kind of groups of those changes are, but very happy to provide the committee with with more detail on the legal advice around what those provisions need to achieve. So this would be in addition, not just a tack on, Yep, thank you. And I hope the chair would welcome, yes,

we would, yeah. Any additional information, you know, Clarke's office will be in touch. Thank you. Dr Megan woods, thank you. Thank you and congratulations for finding a another channel to talk about the rate payer assistance scheme. Well done. Questions. Do you see that would need any other specific enabling provisions within this? Or do you think that we could pick that up through raise amendments?

So the first thing around the ratepayers assistance scheme is it won't be including vehicles. This is property contingent finance to tie two vehicles. So I think that's an important thing to notice. I haven't actually thought too much into I'll have to come back to you on that one where there's anything specific in this bill for enabling vehicle to grid. I haven't, Dave, I'm not sure if you've got any thoughts on that. And I'm not just to clarify. I'm not thinking about including the vehicle part of the vehicle, vehicle to grid. I'm thinking if there's infrastructure that's needed to go at the kind of the grid side of the vehicle, vehicle to grid. Yeah,

so the proposed rate payer assistance scheme and these enabling provisions we're seeking would absolutely enable in the future, vehicle grid chargers to be brought in. We're not proposing that they be brought in as part of the first package of technology, but absolutely cabinet and local government together could take a decision to bring culture grid chargers themselves into the scheme in the future.

Yeah, and just my second question, Mr. Chair, that you, you talk about you talk about it at high level. I think one of the things that it's really worth emphasising is what this could do in terms of foregone infrastructure costs, either at the transmission or distribution level, and how that would translate into savings for consumers in terms of reducing those lines charges? Yeah, sure, so the I was really happy when I could put out a recent study on just flexibility, which is a technical term for customers doing things a little bit differently, but flexibility will say, you know, according to Ike, around $3 billion in infrastructure costs, which I think is super important. I think that is way too conservative. And we've got numbers to suggest that, more than that, the simple thing is, is that if you can relieve the upfront capital cost burden for customers they will invest in their properties. Generation is obviously one thing on the rooftop that will bring down prices, but having batteries, a battery in a home, removes that home from and very, very quickly you end up in a situation where you do not have to keep building wires, poles and wires to handle those winter morning and winter afternoon loads, because the battery the household will be running off its battery, rather than off the poles and wires. And that means, you know, the average utilisation rate of poles and wires in New Zealand sits at about 40% which means, conceivably, we can squeeze twice as much electrons into the same poles and wires if we figure out how to smooth out those peaks. And household peaking is the number one, number one cause of peak congestion on our poles and wires. A battery in a home, or a battery in lots of homes, will do a signature have a significant impact on future infrastructure

costs. Thank you. And through you, Mr. Chair, if, if you've got alternative modelling around what they've saved for the foregone cost of infrastructure, I think the committee, it would be really useful for the committee to see that while considering, yeah, I mean, what I can add in here is that it's something like Australia's deployed 200,000 batteries in six months. That is more peaking power than the entirety of the Huntley power station. That is such a topical thing in New Zealand's energy sector. So it just shows you a lot of a little as a lot, yeah, and working through, working through this bill. If this bill can enable a lot of a little being a loss, that's a huge outcome for New Zealand's infrastructure bills moving forward.

Thanks. Mike, thanks, Dave, Okay, anyone else for rewiring R tou road? That's good, Dave and Mike, okay. Thank you, gentlemen. Thank you. Thanks for appearing. Thanks everyone. Good to see you. Okay, we are running well ahead of time. We got anyone else queued up, or as we might have to have a break just to catch up our NZ initiative. Are here, fantastic. Okay, we're ready to roll. Then look. I can see Eric. I.

Okay, okay. Well, thank you. Thank you for being here well ahead of time, because we've had a couple of you're also just when members thought they were going to cup of coffee. It's something much more enjoyable. So welcome. I don't know if we're more enjoyable than coffee. I suppose it depends on the quality of the coffee here, but thank you, Mr. Chair, and it's always great showing up early so I can hear folks like rewiring our TI ora. It's always fun to chat with them and hearing how they see opportunities in here. I will kind of lead off, and then I'll punt to my colleague, Dr Beno blasky, who's done the main work on this for us, and who has a report forthcoming that we'll be sending to the committee as soon as it's ready, it's going through some more editorial and review processes, but it gets into a lot more depth at a high level, some of what rewiring arteriole was talking about, leads into what we see as potential opportunities for improvement here. So we would love the first tremendously important work, and it's part of these bike kind of cross party push towards enabling more housing. It's critically important for that, as well as for other work, we would love some augmentations to this to make sure that it can achieve its full potential, particularly private initiation of some of these IFF debt vehicles, democratic ascent mechanisms, to make sure that everybody's happy with the levies that are put on their properties for the projects that are being undertaken, that they see the projects as being worthwhile. Some of what we see in the legislation and in the prior frameworks has maintained a cabinet check on a lot of this in lieu of having that kind of democratic ascent mechanism for the levied properties. And if I start thinking about projects for larger scale battery schemes, it would be neat to have to enable communities to come up with these arrangements to pitch them as a project where there's a levy on the property that funds, that provide the fine, the funding for the debt that finances the project. But you also have the ascent of those whose properties are being levied for the project, because that helps ensure that well, that it passes its own kind of benefit cost assessment that in the view of those who are levied for the project, the project is worthwhile. I'll shut up and pun to Benno,

thank you. So a board position is that we support the bill. Having said that, we're not here to take issue with anything that's in the bill, what I'm here to do is to talk about what may not be there. And so the key message is that the bill should be the beginning of a journey, not a destination. The original purpose for the very idea of this type of model was to create a second alternative, or you could say, in this case, additional finance system that runs in parallel to the one we already have. And it was the it was conceived as part of the overall effort to achieve housing affordability and to change the dynamics in land markets. So basically, the this model, or let's say, not necessarily this current model, but the model that was meant to come into existence, and that this one is starting to move towards, is, we could say, a necessary precondition. It's not optional. And what I want to say is that the evolution, the further evolution, of this model, is also not optional. So what we have currently is not enough and not sufficient, but it's also the best I suspect we can do right now, and the changes that are being made are good, and those are the types of first steps I would also take. But what we would like to advocate for is some additional steps so that a greater strategic posture can be taken in relationship to this work, and that strategic posture is to, as I said, to develop that kind of alternative finance system in a broader sense. And I want to talk a little bit more about that. So I'd like to start with Infocom, that put out its national infrastructure plan, and its basic conclusion is that we need to do better with what we've got in our existing, current system, not only that, but it's also told us that we spend maximally in the OECD, but we also maximally inefficient. And basically what they're saying, in my point of view, is that we're bleeding money, and not only that, we're bleeding money, but that looking ahead, in the future, we're going to reach multiples of our GDP, that we it's not sustainable and that we won't be able to have the services. For example, the older generation, those retiring, won't have the services they need, and the younger generations, also, my generation, won't have what it needs to transform land markets and get housing affordability. So that's basically what I took from the Infocom and. Made some recommendations about what to do with the existing with our existing system, and it pointed towards this alternative system in its in its work, it started talking about alternative ways of financing infrastructure, particularly for different types of projects like growth infrastructure, and through doing that, to basically alleviate the pressure on the existing basic finance channel that we do have, so that that finance channel can work to actually to deliver other types of infrastructure, particularly the ones that, for example, the older generation renewals and those types of things that are going to need to be done. So basically freeing up capacity. So for me, that this work, this particular work, has generational stakes, both for younger future generations, but also the existing generation that's ageing. So what I'm saying is that we need two systems. One is the existing finance system, and the one that this model is trying to create. This model, however, doesn't achieve it yet. I think that's a critical thing. And I would like to talk a little bit about that and how was originally conceived. The existing system you could conceive as our name, at the central channel, as a pie. It can't go very fast. It's like a wage earner. It gets rates and it has basically a cap. The ability to raise debt and ability to raise further debt is limited by its ability to get a higher wage, not only that, but the existing pie, I would maybe say, is not used very efficiently, and the efficiency incentives are very low. So there's a lot of discussion, for example, in for comms work of how we can improve on efficiency and how we invest in infrastructure and making sure that we don't bleed as much. The other thing, the third aspect of the pie, is that politics is very much involved. So we there's there's a real issue with prioritisation, that a lot that we're paying for is actually not necessarily what we need. Conversely the new or that the alternative fine system actually turns all of those three aspects of the pie around. It works by virtue of growing the pie, and pays off what it creates. By growing that pie, it creates value. It creates very mini streams basis on the basis of that value created. So rather than a wage earner, it's more like an entrepreneur. It creates business or it creates valuable activity, and that valuable activity itself pays its business. Its business. The other one is it has different efficiency incentives, because people that raise the debt, they get involved or put in the equity to develop the project, they have skin in the game. They care about how much they're paying. The third aspect is that investment follows value, and that's also where the the kind of the beneficiary, kind of consent mechanism, comes in play. People are willing to pay for what they consider as valuable, and they'll think hard about whether it is, in fact, variables worth what they're paying for. That is not to say that the central channel is bad. It's needed. We can't do without. But what I'm saying is it's not enough. And I would like to point to the Productivity Commission's report infrastructure funding and fintech in local government, 9219 their conclusion was, effectively that our current rating based framework and our funding system only works in a static environment. That means no growth, no change, we can cope as soon as we go beyond that. For example, if we want to change the nature of the landmark as the planning system, we want to go for growth. We want to serve future generations. When we want to serve our existing generations as services in the future, it will not suffice. That's what their conclusion was, and they, as a consequence, recommended to develop alternative finance vehicles, which is this model?

We've got some recommendations. We're happy to talk about those or to answer broader questions on it. Ben's got a large report that'll be coming. This has been a bit of a preview of some of the stuff that's going to be in there. But thank the committee for pursuing this work. It's really important. I think I'd like to thus leave a last thing. The current model still relies on the crown to pack it. The only way to get the alternative finance system we really want is if that gets, I would say, removed. That's the only way that we can get true what we call non recourse finance, and that's where finance is bottom up, enabled, created and authorised and dynamic, and that's the kind of dynamism we need in our land markets. So without that, it's not going to work. And by saying that now it's not, I'm not trying to say, oh, did the crown get out of it? The crown is in there for a reason. That's because our current institutions don't allow non recourse finance. So a broad recommendation is we need a strategic posture and do a few things more in this bill to start the journey, not that this is a destination, to start developing those institutions, to eventually be able to withdraw the GSP, the government support package, so that true non recourse finance becomes really possible in New Zealand. And as a final thing, New Zealand used to finance over 50% of. All this local like infrastructure through this type of mechanism, and we removed it, and the consequences, shortage, yeah, we

did it before we can do it again. Happy to take questions. Okay, good way to finish. Okay, ceiling, right? Brown, thank you. Um, yeah, I just had a question about the type of infrastructure, and this is I'm a guest on this black committee. So I, if it's obvious, I apologise, but I think about the types of infrastructure that should or shouldn't be covered by this, and I particular interest in stormwater management. And sometimes that's both better for the environment, but also more cost effective if it's natural plantings and so on, which need maintenance rather than concrete. Not always, but sometimes, do you see this mechanism potentially be able to fund that and the ongoing

Yeah, I think that we've suggested a mechanism where the ascent of the rated properties determines whether the project should go ahead. Yeah, if a storm water project is viewed by the property owners in the area as far more cost effective than anything else that they could have absolutely so that you'd have a vote of the affected rate payers saying, we want this project, and we're happy to pay an extra $100 or whatever it is on our rates for the next 20 years to cover the cost of it. That kind of flexibility means that whatever comes up from the community that's affected can be authorised without select committee or Parliament having to put an itemised list of these things and not these things. It would be the ascent of the rated properties that says whether the project can go ahead

and you don't see anything in this. Or the bill that It amends that would limit what we might mean by infrastructure. Or the original bill had a Title List of type infrastructure for which it was valid. I was involved in specifying that list, or, like, I was involved in expanding the list because I wanted communities and resilience to be present as well. I personally think that any such limit is a limit, and it may not be necessary. But like, as long as you don't have community assent, it gets more difficult for the accountability, which is why the democratic kind of democratic kind of representative authorising mana is so fundamental. You

need to have an itemised list if you're not going back to the people who are being rated for it, because they might have something imposed on them that they really don't value. But if they say, No, we want this, and it's enthusiastic, open it up. I would like to add here the Infocom did talk about this in the report, that there's types of infrastructure. it starts getting into some complexity around the existing rates, what you're paying for, and a top up on top. But, yeah, thank you. It's good. The only reason I'm asking is because obviously, yeah, you're deliberating right now, yeah. So just it covers how it was created, the model why, how it's evolved, how it's been applied. And it also goes through the history of finance in New Zealand and what we have done in the past. And it basically, it basically explains the intricacies how it works and what was actually meant to be created, and how to get there.

I guess his background, Benno, was with HUD for a while, and Treasury, and with Treasury and with LG NZ, I've been around the traps involved in this issue for like a decade. So with the chairs indulgement, yeah for sure, and we are running a full select committee process with the six months. We're not reporting back to the ninth of June. We extended submissions out till the 20th February, to give the TLA is every opportunity to submit. So yeah, we are all wanting good advice on this, so we're waiting to report. Yeah, and

yeah, I've also got involved in from nifco, that's national infrastructure funding company, and also Treasury that we're present for designing the model. Okay? Anyone else? Dr Wilson, yeah, without having read the report, can you give us some examples? Numbers of what you would call normal recourse finance, where it's coming from, because does this, oh, sure. Doors slightly more nio town, sure. So I'll just give an example from a report that I put out a couple of years ago. I live in candala, a 20 minute walk from my house, is the NIO town hall. There's a plaque on the wall of nio Town Hall explaining that it was built by the NIO progressive Residents Association. They had wanted a town hall. They had a ballot of the local community, and they said, we are happy to pay an extra 20 pounds on our rates for the next 10 years to pay off any debt that the council takes on to build this hall. They took that authorization vote to council. Council puts the levy. The levy provides the fund, the financing. They pay it off over time. Everybody wanted it. They built it themselves. Council wasn't on the hook for it. Now, if the like every if everybody in the community had gone bankrupt, well, then the debt would not have been paid back, but it wouldn't have been Council's debt, right? That's what non recourse means that the properties that are levied are the ones standing behind it. There isn't recourse either to sue the crown, to pay you or council. That keeps it separate, and it means that communities can go ahead and build awesome things without people telling them no if they want to do it.

Another example is the Harbour Bridge and also the general electrification of all of New Zealand. I remember my two shillings, Dr Wilson, two different examples are, I think, instructive. So you had discussion about stormwater, which is not a localised issue. It's a systemic issue across much larger if you like rate take or how it's funded, as opposed to a new park or a new ball or anything else the community get by. How would you address those kind of larger systemic infrastructure programmes of work that need to be happened? And when we're talking about integration councils and thinking more regionally, that becomes even more of an issue to address.

Okay, so we've already gotten an example of Wellington Council imposing a levy through IFF for the sludge plant. People can argue the merits of the sludge plant, but it is a bit of infrastructure that serves a pretty broad community I would have preferred that that go up, go through a democratic ascent process. But it's showing that you can use this mechanism for infrastructure that affects a large range of properties. And the kind of ascent mechanism that was present in the local body loans act 1913 and then amended in 28 as a double majority threshold. You needed a majority of rated properties by number constituting a majority by value. And it makes sure that you're not getting projects that just benefit some small, concentrated group, or that impose a lot of cost on one group rather than another. The community wants the project. It can go ahead, and it could be as broad as a stormwater network or as narrow as a town hall.

Yeah, just to say that this type of mechanism is already in place in policy by Waddington council. So, and that's pretty much a gold standard, and can be just transported, is weed around a little that. Well, the ideas, okay? Dr Megan woods, thank you, and thank you for your submission. Interested in the recommendation. We discussed it at four point team saying Treasury should develop a framework that standardises crown risk across qualifying projects, rather than negotiating terms project by project. I think really interesting suggestion in limiting the total crown exposure just in terms of the standardised framework. Just interested now, would you see that being differentiated by by procurement model and the different ways that different procurement models are costing risk and where they're putting it?

They can, do that. The point is that they have a framework that people can pick up. That's the point. I see. You'd see this, yeah? And that's so the transaction cost of doing the doing the transaction get lowered, yeah? And that means it's cheaper and easier to get bigger projects off the like smaller projects off the ground. So we'd have a bit more transparency there of how a kind of a designer build would be costing the RUS Kei as opposed yep, yep. Standardisation doesn't mean Single, single form, okay? Thank you.

Okay, right? I think anything any other questions? Thank you initiative. No. Thank you gentlemen. Thank you committee for taking the time. Thank you. Okay, have we got his worship. Stan Gordon, hello, Mr. Chair, Mr. Mayor. Great to see you again. As we called you last time, Mayor of the year, I think it was and but we haven't seen you since the. Local body elections, and congratulations on your re election and your clear and strong mandate to continue to lead your beloved district. Okay, also, we've also got Chris online too, so we have got your submission. In fact, AI just showed me that your submissions the only discoverable one on the internet at the moment. So well done. Pass on to your comms team that it's easily found, and we look forward to your presentation.

Good afternoon, Mr. Chair and members of the Select Committee, and thank you for those kind words. And can I just say good luck with your performance review this year. I'm sure you'll do fine noticing the Facebook follower Howick, if you are in your own community. So thank you for those. Can I also acknowledge members of the Select Committee, as well as has been mentioned on the mayor of Waimea kiririri, and with me is Chris Janae, who's the general manager of finance and business support for our council. For those who don't know, waimakiri District Council is the fourth largest territorial authority in the South Island, and we're located north of Christchurch and share boundaries with the city Selwyn and the hanaui districts. We range from urban townships such as Rangi ora and Kaia poi to the remote High Country farms of the Lees Valley. 60% of our resident residents live in the district's four largest towns, and there are six and a half 1000 rural residential lifestyle blocks in the district, as well as still a very strong farming base. District is one of New Zealand's fastest growing when I was elected 22 years ago, we have doubled since that time. We were 38,000 in population. Then we're now at 74,000 and the latest district plan, which we approved last year creates between 14 to 17,000 new sections, and the populations expected to grow over 100,000 in the next 20 years. So we are a fast growing district coming to our council position we support the intent of unlocking lock and growth by the government of prioritising the simplification of the bill. However, we have some concerns of the mechanisms proposed to achieve this. The mechanisms described in the bill appear to shift the burden of infrastructure funding onto ratepayers. We have concerns about how the bill doesn't allow for certainty of infrastructure costs over time. It's also a view of the council that not only new properties should be subject to levies. There also needs to be a mechanism to ensure parity with how infrastructure endorsements are made among others, without adequate transparency as to the certainty of the financial commitments, there are potential risks for ratepayers with this proposed bill. We also believe the bill needs to be clearer that levies are not subject to any rate skipping, because that's obviously something that's being proposed in for an area like ours would be of concern if we're having to take that into account overall, there is some potential overlap between this bill and the development levies proposal, which, when combined, could have significant unintended consequences for ratepayers, and that levies added to rates could become a hidden unexpected cost for homeowners. On top of existing rates and mortgages. Well, thank you for the opportunity to appear today. This is a technical submission approved by the Council under delegation to the Chief Financial Officer. We've included feedback from Hora The Independent Maori Statutory Board, water, care services, limited and two of our local boards. Auckland Council supports the intent of the bill, the SPV model and most of the proposed amendments, our comments are aimed at strengthening safeguards to protect ratepayers and long term infrastructure integrity. This is important to us, because New Zealand's largest city and economic engine, we've got a lot of growth pressures, and there are constraints on financing and funding infrastructure. The SPV model is an important tool to help bridge that gap. So we support expanding the regime, but not expensive long term risk management. So a key issue on that point is that we do support account. Councils can't withhold endorsement where technical requirements are met and removing unnecessary procedural barriers. But we are concerned with removing the requirement for infrastructure endorsement where assets won't be vested in the council or another responsible levy authority, and this is because of infrastructures built to a lower standard and later fails, or the private entity operating it does, there'll be strong public and political pressure for council to step in, and the costs will fall to ratepayers. We want to avoid that, and we've got some real examples of that in the Auckland area. They're mainly wastewater. And of course, the infrastructure issues are more apparent when there are issues with wastewater, but there are risks with us across other infrastructure, which we don't want to fall in the long run on ratepayers. So we think the infrastructure endorsement for all eligible infrastructure should be retained. It's a safeguard, not a barrier. We've also made some comments about protected Maori land, and the current Act provides an opt out for this. We think that opt out should be retained. Most Maori land in Auckland is rural, with most categories of protected land, non raidable, it wouldn't be subject to the levy. Doesn't have a lot of development potential. Would contribute ng levy revenue. So the fiscal and administrative impact of keeping the opt out for large levy areas be pretty immaterial. Some key points we'd like to make in regard to water care, speaking on behalf of Angela at this point, Angela is the Chief Financial Officer at water care. Water care supports the bill and the SPV framework. However, the bill assumes all the water organisations will charge under the local government Water Services Act, and water care doesn't charges contractually, and we note that without amendment, water care's ability to act as a responsible levy authority is legally unclear. So water care supports being able to be a responsible levy authority, but the bill needs to accommodate their charging systems. But in addition, the bill should also provide for a territorial authority to act as a responsible levy authority for water and wastewater assets. This would allow infrastructure investment where there's more than one type of infrastructure to be done together and levied together, but also because the territorial authority can do rates type levies, which provide a more secure revenue stream than water billing type arrangements, and that may therefore be more attractive to financiers. So we think that option should be available as well. The Hora submission, which is also attached to ours, is concerned about the slight narrowing of the definition of protected Maori land, and also agrees with our view that protected Maori land should have the option to opt out, irrespective of levy area size. We also note that it's a good idea that the bill provides for special purpose vehicles to apply the levies incident at the time of development, like a development contribution, we feel that that should be extended to targeted rates, to give that same additional flexibility to councils if they are wishing to Do a targeted rate for growth infrastructure. Further, we note that the council charges should retain priority over SPV levies and rating sale situations to ensure that something doesn't go well, it's not falling on ratepayers. So our conclusion points are that we support the intent and the direction of the bill, that the SPV model is an important tool to support growth. Our recommendations are about risk management, equity and the long term protection of ratepayers.

We definitely welcome the amendment that will allow IFF levies to be taken in conjunction with development contributions for specific projects. This was an issue when we set up our transport IFF Levy, and there were definitely one or two projects we'd already taken a small amount of DCs for that we couldn't include in that levy for that reason that we otherwise probably would have. We certainly welcome the amendments to the protected Maori land provisions. These were a real challenge with our transport IFF Levy, because it's just next to impossible to identify protected Maori land. And in Tauranga, we have a huge amount of protected Mairi land, including a significant amount that's located within our current and future urban growth areas where we have significant infrastructure requirements, funding requirements, and would certainly like to be able to look to use wh levees. At the moment, we really can't, because of the way the PML provisions are structured, but moving forward, and particularly with the exemption for IFF levies that have more than 5000 levyable units, we think that's going to really help with some of our future growth areas like Te tou mu, we Do question whether that limit could be, why it's 5000 and whether it could be a bit lower, that would probably open things up further, but nonetheless, it's a very positive change. There are a couple of issues we still have regarding PML, and I'm not the expert on those, so if you have questions, probably Direct your nose to Ben would be best. We can't accurately do that, and we and the likely effect is that some protected Mairi land will be mis categorised as non protected Mairi land. Our proposal was to establish a remission system aligned with the rating act, placing the burden of proof on the protected Mairi land owners, which would create a more accurate and effective method to deal with protected Maori land. Secondly, we have instances in our city where Maori land has been developed on a lease hold basis by commercial operators. So we have some very large retirement villages over two or 300 units, and some very large industrial developments. I won't name names, but including the likes of large transport operators on sites that might be sort of five hectares or larger. So that's the proposal, is to widen that to the likes of ng ti water service entities and developers. And we think that makes sense, but particularly in the case where developers are using it. We think that the, I guess, the ability of councils to, I guess, raise issues or concerns, is quite limited. All of that infrastructure will create a significant burden on councils, and that burden really starts from day one, when that infrastructure is vested, particularly the requirement. The local government required requirement to fund depreciation. So often, the costs come up front, but the revenues from additional rates come much more incrementally over time, and that's concerning in a rates capping environment that we're moving into. So we just think, from our perspective, there should be a broader ability for councils to provide its views where IFF proposals are being proposed by other parties. I mean, ultimately, decision making will sit with the minister, which is appropriate, but it will allow for better informed decision making of the potential implications of those sort of transactions. Not sure if you heard this one yet, but the old chestnut of GST generally. Our understanding is that financial transactions are GST exempt. But IFF levies are not, and the GST component does affect, I guess, their competitiveness is in cost as a funding tool. So we would, I guess, urge you guys to consider whether GST should be removed or allow for some sort of rebate on that. Ultimately, it's going to increase the use of IFF as a funding tool, which in turn will deliver more infrastructure and more development and ultimately more GST revenue for government, I guess in my my assessment, yeah, this one is a wee bit tricky. But through our transport IFF levy that we have in place, it's it delivers like a large scale, sort of 10 year programme of transport investments around the city. A lot of those are co funded by NZ TA, and given the changes that we have is if governments change as transport priorities and funding availability changes as business case processes develop, sometimes that funding falls away and we we've ended up in a position where we were unable to proceed, or some of our transport projects are delayed, that we're actually charging a levy for. And and so there's a misalignment between ng ti kind of planning cycles and certainty and the ability to use IFF levies for transport projects. And so that would be something we would we would seek to be considered and looked at in the least. What we would like to see is the ability for us to amend an order in council for a levy after it has been established if one or more of those projects in it are delayed or cannot be delivered, and with the potential for replacing other projects into the levy to enable that levy to be used efficiently, those are all my key points. Hopefully I haven't taken too much time, but I'll leave it there for any questions you may have.

And look at a high level. Our position is this, we support the intent of the bill to improve the accessibility and the usability of infrastructure funding and financing framework. However, unless some of the key areas, the complexity and some of the uncertainty and governance are addressed, the framework risks remaining unutilized in the infrastructure deficit that it was designed to solve or persist. And I guess, look, we saw this long standing infrastructure funding and financing in the original Act back in 2020 we thought was designed to try and respond to that challenge. And look, The Property Council was supportive of that, and in practice it should have, you know, should have sped it up. But look, there's been some difficulties in getting it operational. And look, the uptake to it is. Been somewhat limited, and I guess that's why we're here today. Look, the amendment bill has the opportunity to direct, to reset now, thinking the trajectory of it, but to be successful, the framework needs to be simpler, needs to be more transparent and align with development realities. And key to that, look, there's five key focus areas that we are interested in, sort of covered, but I think we'll just call those out. Look, reducing some of the complexities, looking at the beneficiary identification and legitimacy in brownfield areas, touching on the governance and coordination, and look safeguards around funding integrity. Look, if I take you to the first one of those reducing the complexity under the bill the levy proposes still requires bespoke negotiations and drafting of SPV documentation. And look for well for well resourced projects, that's quite manageable, but for many of the mid sized projects, as we go around the country, or the bundled projects, these fixed transactional costs remain prohibitive. In our view. Our members consistently report that uncertainty around the legal costs and undertaking under the current regime deters lenders and increased project risks, and ultimately that flows through into housing cost and into commercial pricing. We recommend that look, there's a stand a statutory obligation for the monitoring body to publish a suite of standardised and tiered templates. And look, this is not hard stuff, but to get in there and publish a suite of documents. It's a model of the levies and the proposals, a standard form, SPV, Constitution, the levy administration, documentation, some simple financial models that can be followed, and some simple beneficiary and cost analysis allocation templates that's that sort of material as a standard document should reduce down some of the complexity, and like standardising it, it reduces the duplication, It lowers the transactional cost, and it gives the council and developers greater certainty right at the outset. Look secondly around beneficiary and cost allocation. The bill requires levies to be imposed on beneficiaries, but provides minimal guidance on how beneficiaries should be identified and our beneficiaries quantified the costs that should be allocated, and who sits in the first and second waves. And look, without some clear methodology, there's some risk of inconsistency, you know, opaque treatment and potentially some unequitable levy outcomes. And you know, as we all know, the last thing we want to do is have risk in this. And the last thing you know, developers great at litigation. You want to reduce down that there's a clear set of beneficiary allocations, and then reduce down the litigation risk. And just having that clarification through it, who the beneficiary is, how the costs are allocated makes it nice and clear, everybody's on board, and it's super transparent. Look, thirdly, legitimacy of mechanisms and brownfields and look, brownfields are super tricky. Brownfields are in the area where levies may be imposed on existing land owners for infrastructure upgrades that they didn't anticipate. And lots of people will get a surprise from this. And while there's an opt in mechanism that's been introduced, which is super helpful, without a clear, democratic, legitimate mechanism in there, there's a risk of objections, and look in a thought to try and reduce that, we recommend supplementing maybe some form of opt in process, whether there's either a ballot based approval model or acquiring a double majority on properties or on A capital value. And then you've got some real strength that you can drive home these brownfield development levy or levies, as they are, and there's some real buy in that you've got the majority there. And where you've reached those thresholds are met, the minister should be required to treat that levy as supported. So you've got a large majority. It's now supported, and then it's driven home. Look fourth item that we had, cross agency, coordination and governance, I personally have said in some of these meetings with with. The IFF projects, you've got multiple agencies, you've got regional levy authorities, you've got a monitoring body, you've got nifga, you've got treasury, you've got local government funding agencies. You've got the audit entity and the traffic entity there. And then sometimes you throw Kainga ora into the mix, the bill explains who can be the regional levy authority, but it does not clarify how coordination should occur. And I guess what we're trying to do is a look towards, if we can reduce down that poor coordination, the delays that it causes, the costs and the delivery risk, we'd recommend that there's some sort of formal guidance in there for cross agency roles, the information sharing, the expectation and the time frames, because, look, we all want this to go quickly, faster, quicker, sooner, and that there's some leads in there that drive it. And I guess we note that it excludes the private sector expertise, and that's sort of, it's a bit tricky, you know, we would recommend that governance is best practice standard and encourage the private sector representation. And that will sort of help, you know, if you've got the private sector on board with it, it helps with, you know, transparency, it may help with procurement, and certainly with some of the developers are actually bearing the most significant risk. You know, ideally you want them on board. You know, 99% of us live in houses that are built by developers,

right? David, I'll just have to get you to wrap up, only because one other and coming, and we've got question time too. Sorry, no, no, that's fine. Water New Zealand's interest is around broadening the scope of this these mechanisms to allow water service organisations so CCOs that have been set up to deliver water services around the country to be able to access this the mechanisms and that they can be responsible levy authorities. So just to provide a little bit of context, the water service delivery plans that were submitted by council in September, by councils submitted in September last year, under legislation previously considered by this committee, have signalled $48 billion that needs to be invested in water infrastructure over the next 10 years. Now, when we look at it, a considerable proportion, more than 50% of that 48 billion is going to be delivered by CCOs. Whether you know how much of that, more than 50% is going to be appropriate to be using these mechanisms, I don't know, but they essentially the points that I just want to be able to make are on our reading the bill appears to be time bound and refers to assets that are transferred from council to CCOs, and we think this these mechanisms ought to be able to apply to new, not yet built infrastructure. Second, that the levy powers should be flexible. The levy arrangements that are in the bill that relate to territorial authorities are. Broad. We think that the the arrangements that would relate to water organisations should be similarly broad. And then finally, that there should be an alignment between the right to assess and to invoice levies under the local Ward under the local government Water Services Act and this bill.
